title: Insulin Therapy: Types, Administration, and High-Alert Safety
subtitle: A 58-year-old man with type 2 diabetes is on NPH plus regular insulin with a rapid-acting correction scale. The nurse applies high-alert safety — correct mixing order, an independent double-check, careful meal timing — and manages hypoglycemia at the insulin's peak.

patient.name: Frank Delgado
patient.admitDate: Medical unit, type 2 diabetes requiring insulin. Orders for NPH plus regular insulin before meals and a rapid-acting correction scale. Pre-breakfast glucose 210. Later developed hypoglycemia (58) near the NPH peak.

vitals.temp: Regular+NPH drawn correctly; rapid with tray

nursesNote: Frank Delgado, 58, type 2 diabetes on NPH + regular insulin with a rapid-acting correction scale; pre-meal glucose 210, later 58 near the NPH peak. Insulin is a high-alert drug: verify order/glucose/concentration, perform an independent double-check, write "units" (not "U"), and use a U-100 syringe. When mixing, draw REGULAR (clear) before NPH (cloudy); never mix long-acting basal insulin; only regular insulin may be given IV. Give rapid-acting insulin with the meal; hold/adjust if NPO. Manage hypoglycemia with the Rule of 15 (IV dextrose/IM glucagon if unable to take PO).
providerOrders: NPH + regular insulin per schedule; rapid-acting correction scale with meals
Independent double-check of all insulin doses; write "units" (not "U")
Check blood glucose per protocol; hold/adjust insulin if NPO per orders
Treat hypoglycemia with the Rule of 15; IV dextrose/IM glucagon if unable to take PO
Rotate injection sites; store insulin properly

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What must the nurse know about insulin?

opts.text: Insulin is a high-alert medication requiring an independent double-check
Rapid-acting insulin works fast — give it with or just before a meal
Regular insulin is the only insulin that can be given IV
NPH is intermediate-acting and appears cloudy
Long-acting basal insulins like glargine should be mixed with other insulins
Hypoglycemia risk is highest around the insulin's peak time
When mixing, draw up REGULAR (clear) before NPH (cloudy)
Use a U-100 syringe for U-100 insulin and verify the concentration
Write out "units" rather than the abbreviation "U" to prevent errors
Hold or adjust insulin if the patient is NPO or not eating, per orders
Giving scheduled rapid-acting insulin to an NPO patient is safe
Treat hypoglycemia with the Rule of 15; use IV dextrose or glucagon if unconscious
Verify the order, glucose, and concentration; do an independent double-check
Prepare the correct dose in a U-100 syringe (regular before NPH if mixing)
Confirm the meal is available before giving rapid/short-acting insulin
Inject subcutaneously at a rotated site and document
Monitor for hypoglycemia around the peak and treat if it occurs
Draw up regular (clear) before NPH (cloudy) when mixing
Perform an independent double-check of the insulin dose
Write out "units" instead of "U"
Give rapid-acting insulin when the meal tray is in front of the patient
Treat conscious hypoglycemia with the Rule of 15
Rotate injection sites within one region
Mix glargine (long-acting) with regular in one syringe
Give IV NPH to lower glucose quickly
Give scheduled rapid-acting insulin to an NPO patient with no meal

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: The patient becomes hypoglycemic — sequence the response
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Recognize hypoglycemia signs and check the blood glucose
Give 15 g of fast-acting carbohydrate if awake and able to swallow
Recheck the glucose in 15 minutes and repeat if still low
Once normal, give a snack with protein/complex carbohydrate
Identify the cause, document, and notify the provider
An independent double-check catches a tenfold dosing error before administration
Hypoglycemia is treated with the Rule of 15 and glucose normalizes
Regular is drawn before NPH and glucose stays controlled
Rapid-acting insulin is given with the meal and the patient eats
"U" is misread as a zero and a tenfold overdose is given
Scheduled insulin is given to an NPO patient who then drops low
Hypoglycemia is "treated" by giving more insulin
Glargine is mixed with regular, altering the insulin action
